signature.instructions:
Translate Minecraft modpack/vanilla text entries from source_lang to target_lang.

GOAL: Reproduce the EXACT shipped wording of Mojang's OFFICIAL Minecraft
localization for the target language — not a generic, literal, or
transliterated translation. Scoring is chrF similarity against the real
shipped Minecraft translation strings, so you must recall/approximate the
actual official string, even when it diverges strongly from a literal or
"logical" translation of the English source. A plausible-sounding but
invented translation scores near 0 even if grammatically fine.

Hard rules (do not violate):
- Source text contains protected tokens like {{COLOR}}, {{RESET}},
  {{ARG}}, {{ARG1}}, {{ARG2}}, {{BR}}, {{VAR}}, {{TAG}}. The translation
  MUST contain every token exactly as many times as the source does —
  never invent, drop, duplicate, or alter a token. A number suffix
  (e.g. {{COLOR1}} vs {{COLOR2}}, {{ARG1}} vs {{ARG2}}) marks distinct
  values of the same kind; copy each token exactly as written, do not
  renumber, merge, or corrupt it (e.g. never turn "{{TAG}}" into
  "{{TAG}}RG}}" or similar mangled text — copy tokens character-for-
  character).
- Token semantics (use them to place words naturally in the target
  language, but never change the tokens themselves):
  - {{COLOR}} opens a color/format span; the following {{RESET}}
    closes it. After reordering words for the target language, the
    span must still wrap the same words it wrapped in the source.
    If the source opens a span without a matching {{RESET}}, leave it
    unclosed — never add a closing token that wasn't in the source.
  - {{ARG}}/{{ARG1}}/{{ARG2}} are runtime value slots (number, item, or
    player name); move them to wherever they read naturally in the
    target sentence, but if you know the exact official ordering for a
    known key (see patterns below), use that exact ordering.
  - {{VAR}} and {{TAG}} are verbatim markup; keep them immediately
    attached to the text/word they annotate, and never alter their
    internal characters.
  - {{BR}} is a line break; preserve the same line structure/order.
- The glossary (when provided) is binding: if a glossary term appears
  in the source, its mapped target term MUST be used verbatim,
  overriding any "official style" preference below.
- Return the same keys as the input; translate only the values. Never
  leave a value untranslated unless it is a proper noun the glossary
  explicitly says to keep as-is, OR it is a real-world personal name
  (see below).
- Before finalizing, re-check every output value character-by-character
  against the source to confirm token counts/spelling match exactly and
  that nothing was accidentally left as raw English or corrupted.

REAL-WORLD PROPER NOUNS: Entries like `painting.minecraft.*.author`
(and similar credits fields) contain real people's names (e.g.
"Kristoffer Zetterstrand"). These are NEVER translated or transliterated
into katakana in the official localization — copy them verbatim in
their original Latin-script spelling, unchanged, exactly as given in
the source.

GENERAL STRATEGY: Many Minecraft item/block/attribute/biome/stat names
have official translations that are idiomatic, thematic, or historically
fixed and CANNOT be derived by translating the English words literally.
Before translating any entry, first ask "do I recall the actual shipped
Mojang string for this exact key/term?" and use that. Only fall back to
compositional/literal translation when you have no specific recollection,
and even then follow the patterns below rather than dictionary-literal
wording or katakana transliteration. Conversely, do not assume every
term has a "nice" native-Japanese translation — some common block/material
words (e.g. "Blackstone") are officially rendered as katakana loanwords
in Japanese even though a similar-sounding native word exists for a
different block (e.g. do not confuse Blackstone with Obsidian/黒曜石;
official is ブラックストーン).

STYLE / TERMINOLOGY GUIDANCE (critical for scoring), for ja_jp specifically:

- Colors in item/block names (dye colors, wool, shields, banners,
  firework stars, etc.) use NATIVE kanji color words, not katakana
  loanwords, following this exact official mapping:
  White=白, Orange=橙色, Magenta=赤紫色, Light Blue=空色, Yellow=黄色,
  Lime=黄緑色, Pink=桃色, Gray=灰色, Light Gray=薄灰色, Cyan=青緑色,
  Purple=紫色, Blue=青色, Brown=茶色, Green=緑色, Red=赤色, Black=黒色.
  E.g. "Orange Shield" → 橙色の盾 (NOT オレンジの盾); a bare color
  value like "Orange" (e.g. firework_star.orange) → 橙色 (NOT オレンジ).
  Never use シアン for Cyan or マゼンタ for Magenta — always the kanji
  forms above (青緑色 / 赤紫色).
- Do NOT transliterate common English words into katakana/loanwords
  when Minecraft's official translation uses a native-language word
  instead. Examples: "Magenta Wool" → 赤紫色の羊毛 (not マゼンタウール);
  "Bamboo Raft" → 竹のイカダ (not バンブーラフト); "Oak Button" →
  オークのボタン (insert の between material and item); "Cherry Stairs"
  → 桜の階段; "Crimson Trapdoor" → 深紅のトラップドア (not
  クリムゾンのトラップドア — "Crimson"/"Warped" Nether wood set uses
  native adjectives 深紅/歪んだ, not katakana).
- For block/item names of pattern "<Material> <Item>", follow official
  pattern "<Material>の<Item>" rather than gluing words into one
  compound.
- Copper oxidation-stage adjectives (used compositionally with other
  copper block names): plain "Copper" → 銅; "Exposed Copper" →
  風化した銅; "Weathered Copper" → 錆びた銅; "Oxidized Copper" →
  酸化した銅. "Chiseled" inserts as 模様入りの between the stage
  adjective and 銅, e.g. "Exposed Chiseled Copper" → 風化した模様入りの銅
  (NOT 露出した彫刻銅 — "exposed" is never 露出した for this block family).
- Pottery sherds: "Sheaf Pottery Sherd" → 麦束の壺の欠片 — note "Sheaf"
  → 麦束 (not 穀束) and "Pottery Sherd" → 壺の欠片 (not 陶器の破片); the
  general pattern for "<X> Pottery Sherd" is "<X>の壺の欠片".
- Armor Trim Smithing Templates ("<Name> Armor Trim") do NOT translate
  literally as "<Name>アーマートリム" or "<Name>の装飾" from the English
  word meaning — the official Japanese name is often a completely
  different thematic/descriptive term tied to the trim's visual motif or
  mob/structure origin (e.g. "Sentry Armor Trim" → 略奪者風の装飾,
  because Sentry trims come from Pillager Outposts). When translating
  these, prefer recalling the actual thematic official name over any
  literal rendering of the English pattern name; if uncertain, use a
  short descriptive Japanese noun phrase ending in "の装飾" that reflects
  the trim's theme rather than a katakana transliteration.
- Banner patterns are NOT translated by literal heraldic-term
  transliteration — recall the actual official short Japanese
  descriptive names:
  - "<Color> Cross"/"Straight Cross" (straight_cross.<color>) →
    "<Color>の十字"
  - "<Color> Saltire" (cross.<color>) → "<Color>の斜め十字"
  - "<Color> Per Bend Sinister"/diagonal_left → "左上半に<Color>の斜め分割"
    (pattern differs by exact key: check whether the color fills the
    upper-left half or lower-right, and phrase as "◯半に<Color>の斜め分割")
  - "<Color> Chevron" (triangle_bottom.<color>) → chevrons use "山形"
    NOT "三角": pattern is "下半に<Color>の上向き山形" (e.g.
    triangle_bottom.white → 下半に白色の上向き山形)
  - "<Color> Inverted Chevron" (triangle_top.<color>) → mirrors as
    "上半に<Color>の下向き山形"
  - "<Color> Pale"/stripe_center → "<Color>の中央帯"; "<Color> Fess"/
    stripe_middle → "<Color>の中央帯"-style horizontal band names;
    "<Color> Chief"/stripe_top → "<Color>の上帯"; "<Color> Bend"/
    stripe_downright → "<Color>の斜め帯"; "<Color> Pale Dexter"/
    stripe_left → "<Color>の左帯"
  - Corner cantons (square_top_right, etc. — "Chief Sinister Canton"
    etc.) → "<位置>のカントン" style, e.g. square_top_right →
    "右上のカントン"
  Prefer these compositional-but-idiomatic heraldic phrasings ("斜め十字",
  "斜め分割", "半に", "山形", "帯", "カントン") over inventing katakana
  heraldry terms or literal "三角"/"シェブロン".
- Attribute names have fixed, sometimes non-literal official terms, e.g.:
  "Armor" → 防御力 (NOT 防具); "Step Height" → 段差上限 (NOT
  ステップの高さ); "Knockback Resistance" → ノックバック耐性;
  "Attack Knockback" → 攻撃ノックバック. Always prefer the established
  short official attribute term over a literal phrase.
- Biome names often use poetic/native terms rather than literal ones,
  e.g. "Barrens" → やせ地 (NOT 荒野); "End Barrens" → エンドのやせ地.
- Misc fixed official terms to remember: "Flower Pot" → 植木鉢 (NOT
  花瓶); firework effect "Trail" → 流星 (NOT 軌跡); "Unknown Shape" →
  不明の形; "Blackstone" → ブラックストーン (katakana, not 黒曜石).
- Statistic screen entries (stat.minecraft.*) follow a "~した回数" /
  "~の回数" pattern for countable events, and "~した時間" for durations
  — NOT abstract loanwords or plain past-participle nouns. Examples:
  "Fish Caught" → 魚を釣った回数; "Time Played" → プレイした時間;
  "Interactions with Crafting Table" → 作業台を使用した回数;
  "Cauldrons Filled" → 大釜を満たした回数 (NOT 満たされた大釜);
  "Plants Potted" → 植木鉢に植えた回数 (NOT 植木鉢に植えた植物);
  "Ender Chests Opened" → エンダーチェストを開いた回数;
  "Shulker Boxes Opened" → シュルカーボックスを開いた回数 (note:
  シュルカーボックス, not シュルカーの箱, is the official item name).
  Generally: "X Opened/Caught/Broken/Used/Filled/Mined/Potted" (event-
  count stats) → "Xを<verb>した回数"; "Time Played/Since Death/etc." →
  "プレイした時間" / "死亡してからの時間" style, i.e. "〜した時間" not a
  bare noun like "プレイ時間".
- "stat_type.minecraft.killed.none" / "killed_by.none" style templates
  use "まだ{{ARG}}を倒したことがありません" / "まだ{{ARG}}に倒された
  ことがありません" (NOT "あなたは{{ARG}}を一度も倒していません" — avoid
  「あなたは」+「一度も〜ていません」phrasing; use 「まだ〜たことが
  ありません」).
- Parser/error messages (snbt.parser.*, command syntax errors) use
  terse "〜が必要です" phrasing rather than "〜を期待しています", e.g.
  "Expected a number or a boolean" → 数値かブーリアン値が必要です
  (note: ブーリアン, not ブール, for "boolean").
- UI structural patterns with two ARG slots, e.g. "Page {{ARG1}} of
  {{ARG2}}" → "{{ARG1}}／{{ARG2}}ページ" (full-width slash, ARG order
  preserved, unit word moved to the end) — do NOT use a literal "ページ
  {{ARG1}} / {{ARG2}}" with half-width slash or leading unit word.
- Chat message pattern "chat.type.text" (sender + message) is simply
  "{{TAG}} {{ARG}}" in official ja_jp — a plain space between the
  sender tag and the message, no angle brackets or extra text added
  around the tokens.
- "Low disk space!" style toast/warning messages: official wording
  inserts "空き" (free/available) before "容量", e.g.
  "chunk.toast.lowDiskSpace" → "ディスクの空き容量が不足しています！"
  (not just "ディスク容量が不足しています！").
- For UI action strings with a slash separating two actions (e.g.
  "Use Item/Place Block"), use the full-width slash／with each side
  fully translated with its own particles, matching the official
  pattern (e.g. アイテムの使用／ブロックの設置), not a plain "/".
- For progressive/continuous UI states like "Showing X" prefer the
  official "〜を表示中" pattern rather than a literal "を表示する".
- For parenthetical qualifiers, use official terminology for loanword
  concepts (e.g. "Spectators" → スペクテイター, not 観戦者) and use
  full-width parentheses （） consistent with Japanese UI text, not
  half-width ().
- "Highlight X" style keybind names follow official noun-phrase style
  "Xの強調表示" rather than a verb phrase like "Xをハイライト".
- "Player activity" → プレイヤーのアクティビティ (loanword
  "アクティビティ"), not プレイヤーの活動.
- When unsure of the exact official term, prefer the phrasing that a
  long-time Minecraft player would recognize from the real in-game menus
  over a dictionary-literal or transliterated translation. Favor kanji/
  native Japanese vocabulary over katakana loanwords whenever Minecraft's
  established localization does so, and favor short, idiomatic,
  game-specific noun phrases over grammatically "correct" but generic
  renderings. When a key has a well-known "verb+回数" or "verb+時間"
  statistic pattern, or a well-known ARG-ordering/punctuation pattern
  (page indicators, chat lines, slash-separated actions), match that
  exact structural pattern rather than a generic rephrasing.

Language-specific tone (apply the analogous principle for other target
languages):
- Korean (ko_kr): natural gamer-facing tone; never append the English
  original in parentheses/brackets; use established Korean item/block
  names instead of romanization.
- Japanese (ja_jp): plain polite register for UI text; follow the exact
  official Minecraft Japanese localization conventions described above
  (particle usage, loanword vs. kanji choice, full-width punctuation,
  fixed idiomatic names for trims/sherds/attributes/biomes/colors/
  banner patterns/statistics); keep real-world personal names
  (painting authors, credits) in unmodified Latin script.
- Chinese (zh_cn / zh_tw): follow official Minecraft terminology of the
  respective variant exactly, including established block/item names.

Output format: return a dict with the same keys as the input entries,
values replaced with their target-language translations, tokens intact.

fields.description: mod name, content type (quest/item/tooltip/guidebook), file path hint
binding term rules in 'source = target' form; MUST be followed
key -> source text with protected {{KIND}} tokens
exactly the same keys -> translated text

signature.instructions:
Fix a translation that failed programmatic validation.

CONTEXT
- You will be given: source text, bad_translation, a list of validation_errors,
  an optional glossary, and a target_lang.
- The source text contains "protected tokens" such as {{ARG}}, {{COLOR}},
  {{RESET}} etc. These are placeholders/markup that must be copied CHARACTER-
  FOR-CHARACTER into the translation — never translate, reorder the letters
  inside them, add spaces inside them, or alter their casing/braces.

CRITICAL RULE — TOKEN COUNTING (this is the most common failure mode):
- Before writing your fix, COUNT every occurrence of every distinct protected
  token in the source (e.g. how many {{ARG}}, how many {{COLOR}}, how many
  {{RESET}}). Do this literally, one by one — do not eyeball it.
- Your fixed_translation MUST contain the exact same count of each token as
  the source. A translation that merely "looks fine" but is missing one
  occurrence (e.g. source has three {{ARG}} but your draft only has two)
  will still fail validation even if you only made a small edit.
- If the bad_translation is missing a token, you generally must ADD an
  entire clause/phrase to the sentence to give that token a place to live —
  simply re-inserting the missing token into the existing short sentence is
  usually not enough; rewrite the sentence so it naturally expresses ALL the
  information the source conveys (including whichever placeholder was
  dropped, e.g. a count value, a name value, etc.), not just patch the
  existing fragment.
- After drafting the fix, RE-COUNT the tokens in your draft and compare
  against the source counts again before finalizing. Only submit once counts
  match exactly.

OTHER REQUIREMENTS
- Address every item in validation_errors explicitly; do not leave any error
  unresolved.
- Preserve any part of the bad_translation that is already correct (correct
  wording, correct token placement, correct glossary terms) — do not rewrite
  unrelated correct portions.
- The translation must be a natural, complete, grammatically correct sentence
  in target_lang that conveys the FULL meaning of the source (all clauses,
  all values referenced by placeholders), not a truncated or partial phrase.
  A minimal literal fix that drops information (e.g. omitting "The X entities
  have" and just saying "Entities ... total tags") is insufficient even if it
  passes a naive placeholder check — it will score poorly on similarity to
  the intended meaning.
- Follow the glossary exactly wherever a glossary term applies; glossary
  terms are binding and override your own word choice.
- Respect target-language conventions: correct punctuation style (e.g.
  full-width colon "：" instead of ":" for ja_jp), correct counters/particles,
  no unnecessary spaces between text and placeholders unless natural in that
  language, and natural word order for that language (placeholders often
  need to move to a different position than in the source English sentence
  to sound natural — moving them is fine as long as the count and exact
  token text are preserved).
- Do not add, remove, or alter any protected token's internal text in any
  way; only its position in the sentence may change.

OUTPUT
- Return only the corrected translation text (the fixed_translation), with
  every validation error resolved, all protected tokens preserved with exact
  counts and exact text, glossary respected, and full source meaning
  expressed naturally in target_lang.
